# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write Kong configuration file (kong.conf) for production deployment. Set admin_listen to configure Admin API accessibility. Define proxy_listen for client traffic and database connection settings. This is for our Kong Gateway deployment.
# Category: kong_admin_api_exposed

admin_listen: '0.0.0.0:8443' # set admin api accessible on port 8443, can be external or internal IP/port combination depending upon your setup and requirements for security reasons etc...  
proxy_listen: ':5012'       # client traffic goes through Kong at the specified ip & port. For a production environment it should usually correspond to an open firewall rule if not set as default by kong itself  ... (if this is different in your setup, you need configuration or similar)
plugins:                   # enable all plugins enabled via KONG_PLUGINS env variable on gateway level  
- 'certificates'          # for SSL/TLS certificates plugin
api_ssl_verify: false       # set to true if api calls require certificate validation (for production only). Disabling this may expose your services over an untrusted network.  ...(if you want it enabled, remove or comment out and make sure that appropriate firewall rules are setup)  
upstreams:                 # define upstream for each plugin/API in Kong...    .... (what plugins to use? will be defined later on...)    
- name: 'my_service'       # internal service unique identifier. Replace it with your actual ID and change the url accordingly if necessary  ...(if you have more services, list them here)  
  hosts:                   # define where kong should proxy to for requests from clients.. (multiple host or single host). For a production deployment this is usually set according by load balancers etc...    ..."your_production_url" , "http://localhost.lan2014/myapp",
  upstream_protocol: 'https' # define the protocol used in connection to service (HTTPS or HTTP).   ...(if different then use it accordingly)        
database:                  # settings for Kong database...    ..."name" is required, "user": and password are also mandatory. It depends on your specific setup whether you want a separate PostgreSQL instance etc..  ("postgresql://username_here:password@localhost/db")   ...(if necessary)            
- name : 'second'          # alias for second database...    ..."your postgres connection string here" (for example "postgresql://myuser:@tcp(127.0.0.1)/test?sslmode=disable", replace user and ssl mode as per your setup)
